acceptance criteria: scoped notebook and visual audits pass for chapter 2
git diff --check passes for the assigned notebook and artifact subtree
chapter goal: Build a standalone incidence model of projective planes, from perspective drawings to the Fano plane.
computational checks: vanishing point lies on each projected parallel image line
ideal points lie on the drawn line at infinity
Fano point pairs determine one line and line pairs determine one point
Fano counts satisfy n = 2 and n^2 + n + 1 = 7
all generated artifacts exist and have nonzero size
concept inventory: perspective drawing as central projection that preserves point-line incidence
parallel Euclidean directions represented by ideal points on one line at infinity
projective plane as a triple (P, L, I) with unique joins, unique meets, and nondegeneracy
point-line duality by transposing incidence data
Fano plane as the seven-point smallest finite projective plane
finite projective plane order n with n + 1 points per line and n^2 + n + 1 total points and lines
implementation notes: Notebook code is chapter-local. It avoids the generic render_chapter_visuals helper and writes only the chapter artifact subtree.

library routing table.fallback: static SVG generated from the same coordinates
library routing table.representation: synthetic perspective diagram plus residual checks
library routing table.why: precise 2D incidence drawings with labelled residuals

library routing table.fallback: saved PNG perspective diagram and JSON residuals
library routing table.why: moving the eye point is easier to inspect as a slider than as several static panels
the uniform-count proof is a dependency argument, not a metric picture

library routing table.representation: finite incidence table and matrix transpose
library routing table.why: finite projective planes are most testable as point-line tables

proof-visualization strategy: Use a NetworkX dependency graph for Lemma 2.1 through Theorem 2.2 and a finite model checker for the Fano plane.
risks: The curved Fano line is a drawing convention for an abstract incidence line, not a Euclidean circle theorem.
The perspective lab is synthetic and uses no scanned or source-page images.

visual sequence.validation: maximum projected point-line residual and vanishing residual
